title: Chapter 21: Ice and Fury
paragraphs: The first strike of the wave of creatures was like a cliff collapsing onto a glass wall. The air exploded with the screech of tearing chitin, the hiss of acid, and a primal roar.

Kirah took the brunt of it. The giant demon crashed into the ranks of beings from the rift like a black meteor. His claws tore through the creatures' gray shells, splashing corrosive ichor. Kirah did not use destruction magic, fearing he might hit the refugees standing behind him—he tore the monsters with his bare paws, flinging aside those who tried to bypass him from the flank.

To the right of the demon, Dzhanavar moved as a gray, blurred smudge. The werewolf moved with the frightening, lethal grace of an infuriated beast. His double-edged axe whistled through the air, each strike accompanied by the wet crunch of severed limbs.
"Come on, come closer!" Dzhanavar growled, dodging a monster's scythe-like blade and driving his own blade straight into its faceted eyes. "A wolf on the hunt knows no fatigue!"

To the left, Rene led a dance of death. The fox slipped between the monsters' mandibles, elegant and elusive. Her thin, poisoned daggers unerringly found the vulnerable joints on the monsters' necks. She moved not like a warrior on a battlefield, but like a surgeon with two scalpels.

Yashil stood directly behind their backs, holding a rusty iron pipe with two shaking hands. He had neither the strength of a demon, nor the speed of a werewolf, nor the agility of a fox. All he could do was finish off those small parasitic creatures that managed to slip past his friends, protecting the refugees huddled behind them. These were people he had served for seven years—the 'Grey City' he had built with his own hands.

Every swing of the heavy pipe echoed with burning pain in his weak muscles. Green blood covered his face, his breath escaping his chest with a whistle. There were four of them against a never-ending stream from the rift in the heavens.

High above them, on the balcony of the Great Gates, an elven captain stood frozen. Tears of helplessness rolled down his pale face. His warrior-guards stood nearby, crossbows lowered, unable to watch the slaughter. They, the High Elves encased in gold plate, hid behind an impenetrable magical shield, watching as the 'filth of the wastelands'—goblin, demon, werewolf, and fox—gave their lives to protect those the elves had abandoned to die.
This sight broke their pride more painfully than any weapon. For the first time in centuries, their armor seemed to them not a symbol of greatness, but a cage for cowards.
The slaughter at the gates turned into a meat grinder.

Kirah began to give way. The huge demon was covered in smoking acid wounds. One of the creatures managed to drive its bony spike deep into the giant's shoulder. Kirah howled in pain, tearing off the monster's head, but sank to one knee. Dzhanavar, whose fur was matted with blood, missed a glancing blow to his ribs and staggered back with a muffled groan. Rene was breathing heavily, her movements losing their former precision; one of her daggers was broken in half.
The flow from the rift seemed endless.

Yashil stepped forward, shielding the fallen Kirah. The goblin raised his rusty pipe, preparing to take the next blow. In his green eyes, there was no fear. Only the cold, calm confidence that he had done everything right.
And at that moment, when one of the creatures in a leap raised its scythes over the goblin's head...
The air temperature dropped sharply, unnaturally. The ground under the leaping monster's paws was instantly covered with a crust of thick ice.
The flying creature suddenly froze in mid-air, pierced by dozens of long ice spears that burst straight from the frozen ground. The monster disintegrated into frozen shards with a sickening crunch.
Dzhanavar blinked in shock, wiping blood from his face. Rene lowered her broken dagger.

A sharp, piercing cold rolled in a wave across the battlefield, freezing the acidic blood and shackling the monsters' movements. Through the fog and ash, from the dark ravines behind the attackers, came a monotonous, frightening march. The clanking of old, rusted armor and the thud of bones against stone.

From the impenetrable gloom, a legion of the dead emerged onto the battlefield. Skeletons of dwarves, elves, and half-orcs of bygone eras, whose empty eye sockets glowed with a deathly-blue necrotic light. They moved in perfect formation, crashing into the flank of the rift flow without fear, without pain, and without doubt.
Ahead of this legion of death, stepping across the frozen ground, came a tall man in a flawless black velvet cloak. His pale, sculpted face expressed nothing but professional focus.
Lord Valthor stopped twenty paces from the exhausted outcasts. With a wave of his black-leather-clad hand, he sent another cascade of ice spears into the thick of the creatures.
Rene laughed quietly, hysterically, leaning her back against the jade wall of Etcha.
"I told you," the fox whispered. "This bloodsucker loves theatrical effects too much to miss the finale."

The vampire paid no attention to the battle that seethed with new force thanks to his undead. He approached the little goblin who was still clutching his rusty pipe. Valthor looked at the green blood covering Yashil's face, at the wounded demon and the heavily breathing werewolf.

"I told you, goblin, that I would come to see how long your light holds when the heavens tear apart," Valthor's voice was quiet but carried over the clash of steel. "I was sure I would find only your ashes here. I believed that in the face of absolute end, you would turn into the same animals you were trying to save this crowd from."
Valthor shifted his gaze to the refugees—dwarves, women, and children who looked at the goblin with hopeless, frantic gratitude.

"The High Elves closed the gates. They acted like cowards, justifying it with the cold logic of survival," the vampire continued, and a rare, almost genuine half-smile flickered on his pale lips. "And you, with nothing but a fragment of rusty iron, proved to an immortal that there is still something in this world worth saving."
Valthor elegantly, with a slight bow, unsheathed a long, thin blade of blackened steel, its edge emitting a thick frost mist.
"My legion will hold the flanks, Yashil of the Rotten Hollow," the vampire lord said. "But the center remains yours. Show them how those who are not afraid die."
Yashil nodded slowly.
